quizQuestion:
El libro {___} leí es muy bueno.
(The book that I read is very good.)
sentenceTranslation: The book that I read is very good.
explanation: que — most common relative pronoun in Spanish, used for both people and things. El libro que leí = the book that I read. que never changes form (unlike English who/which/that). Used after both animate and inanimate antecedents.

quizQuestion:
La persona {___} llamó es mi amigo.
(The person who called is my friend.)
sentenceTranslation: The person who called is my friend.
grammarPointLabel: relative clause: que (for people, subject position)
explanation: que — used for people when they are the subject of the relative clause. La persona que llamó = the person who called. Note: quien can also be used for people, but que is more common in spoken Spanish.

quizQuestion:
La casa {___} compramos es grande.
(The house that we bought is big.)
sentenceTranslation: The house that we bought is big.
grammarPointLabel: relative clause: que (object of relative clause)
explanation: que — relative pronoun used when the antecedent (la casa) is the object of the relative verb. La casa que compramos = the house that we bought. Unlike English, Spanish never omits que: *la casa compramos is wrong.

quizQuestion:
La persona con {___} trabajo es simpática.
(The person with whom I work is nice.)
sentenceTranslation: The person with whom I work is nice.
grammarPointLabel: relative clause: quien (after preposition, for people)
explanation: quien — used for people, especially after prepositions: con quien, a quien, de quien. La persona con quien trabajo = the person with whom I work. que cannot follow a preposition directly (in standard Spanish).

quizQuestion:
El chico a {___} vi es tu hermano.
(The boy whom I saw is your brother.)
sentenceTranslation: The boy whom I saw is your brother.
grammarPointLabel: relative clause: quien (object of verb with personal a)
explanation: quien — after personal a when referring to people: a quien vi = whom I saw. The personal a before people in object position persists before quien. Contrast: El chico que vi (also correct, but more informal).

quizQuestion:
La ciudad {___} vivo es Madrid.
(The city where I live is Madrid.)
sentenceTranslation: The city where I live is Madrid.
explanation: donde — relative pronoun used for places. La ciudad donde vivo = the city where I live. donde is used when the antecedent is a place. Contrast: en que (more formal, same meaning): la ciudad en que vivo.

quizQuestion:
El restaurante {___} comemos siempre está lleno.
(The restaurant where we always eat is full.)
sentenceTranslation: The restaurant where we always eat is full.
grammarPointLabel: relative clause: donde (for places, non-proper nouns)
explanation: donde — used for any place antecedent, not just cities. El restaurante donde comemos = the restaurant where we eat. donde here = in which. Adverb donde becomes a relative pronoun when it follows a noun antecedent.

quizQuestion:
{___} dices es verdad.
(What you say is true.)
sentenceTranslation: What you say is true.
grammarPointLabel: relative clause: lo que (abstract antecedent — what)
explanation: lo que — used when there is no specific antecedent (equivalent to 'what' = 'that which'). Lo que dices = what you say / that which you say. lo que is the subject or object with no preceding noun. Very common at B1+.

quizQuestion:
No entiendo {___} quieres decir.
(I don't understand what you mean.)
sentenceTranslation: I don't understand what you mean.
grammarPointLabel: relative clause: lo que (object position, embedded clause)
explanation: lo que — lo que quieres decir = what you want to say / what you mean. lo que here is the object of entiendo. In English this is 'what', in Spanish always lo que (not *qué in indirect statements).

quizQuestion:
Haz {___} quieras.
(Do whatever you want.)
sentenceTranslation: Do whatever you want.
grammarPointLabel: relative clause: lo que (whatever = lo que + subjunctive)
explanation: lo que — Haz lo que quieras = Do whatever you want. lo que + subjunctive = whatever. This combines relative pronoun lo que with the present subjunctive for open/indefinite reference. Common in everyday speech.

quizQuestion:
{___} llegar a casa.
(I've just arrived home.)
sentenceTranslation: I've just arrived home.
explanation: acabo de — acabar de + infinitive = to have just done. Acabo de llegar = I've just arrived (moments ago). Formation: conjugated acabar + de + infinitive. This B1 periphrasis precisely expresses recent completion.

quizQuestion:
{___} salir.
(He/she has just left.)
sentenceTranslation: He/she has just left.
explanation: acaba de — él/ella form of acabar + de + infinitive. Acaba de salir = he/she has just left. Present tense: acabo, acabas, acaba, acabamos, acabáis, acaban + de + infinitive. Very natural Spanish for recent events.

quizQuestion:
{___} terminar el proyecto.
(We've just finished the project.)
sentenceTranslation: We've just finished the project.
explanation: acabamos de — nosotros form. Acabamos de terminar = we've just finished. Note: acabamos de (nosotros) vs. acababan de (ellos imperfect) — both frequently used. Imperfect: Acababan de llegar cuando llamé.

quizQuestion:
{___} llamar esta mañana.
(He called again this morning.)
sentenceTranslation: He called again this morning.
explanation: volvió a — volver a + infinitive = to do something again. Volvió a llamar = he called again. Any conjugated form of volver + a + infinitive expresses repetition. Present: vuelvo a, vuelves a... Preterite: volví a, volviste a...

quizQuestion:
{___} intentarlo.
(I'll try again.)
sentenceTranslation: I'll try again.
grammarPointLabel: volver a + infinitive (do again): yo present
explanation: vuelvo a — present tense yo form of volver + a + infinitive. Vuelvo a intentarlo = I'm trying again / I'll try again. Often expresses determination or frustration. Note stem change: volver → vuelvo (o→ue).

quizQuestion:
{___} estudiando español.
(I'm still studying Spanish.)
sentenceTranslation: I'm still studying Spanish.
explanation: sigo — seguir + gerundio = to keep doing / to still be doing. Sigo estudiando = I'm still studying. seguir has a stem change (e→i): sigo, sigues, sigue, seguimos, seguís, siguen. gerundio: estudiando (regular -ar).

quizQuestion:
{___} lloviendo.
(It's still raining.)
sentenceTranslation: It's still raining.
explanation: sigue — él/ella form of seguir + gerundio. Sigue lloviendo = it's still raining. Common in weather expressions. Note: llover → lloviendo (regular -er gerund). Seguir imperfect: seguía lloviendo (it kept raining).

quizQuestion:
{___} trabajando cuando llegué.
(They were still working when I arrived.)
sentenceTranslation: They were still working when I arrived.
explanation: seguían — imperfect of seguir (ellos) + gerundio. Seguían trabajando = they kept working / were still working (ongoing state in past). Imperfect of seguir: seguía, seguías, seguía, seguíamos, seguíais, seguían.

quizQuestion:
{___} tres años estudiando español.
(I've been studying Spanish for three years.)
sentenceTranslation: I've been studying Spanish for three years.
grammarPointLabel: llevar + time + gerundio (have been doing for): yo
explanation: llevo — llevar + time expression + gerundio = to have been doing for [time]. Llevo tres años estudiando = I've been studying for three years. This replaces the English 'have been doing' construction. Amount of time + gerundio.

quizQuestion:
¿Cuánto tiempo {___} esperando?
(How long have you been waiting?)
sentenceTranslation: How long have you been waiting?
explanation: llevas — tú form of llevar. ¿Cuánto tiempo llevas esperando? = How long have you been waiting? The question asks about duration. Answer: Llevo dos horas esperando. llevar (yo→llevo, tú→llevas, él→lleva).

quizQuestion:
{___} dos horas trabajando.
(He's been working for two hours.)
sentenceTranslation: He's been working for two hours.
explanation: lleva — él/ella form. Lleva dos horas trabajando = he's been working for two hours. Pattern: Subject + lleva/llevas/llevo... + time expression + gerundio. The time expression comes between llevar and gerundio.

quizQuestion:
{___} una semana aquí.
(We've been here for a week.)
sentenceTranslation: We've been here for a week.
explanation: llevamos — nosotros form. Llevamos una semana aquí = we've been here for a week. Note: in this case no gerundio follows because 'estar aquí' is implied, not an active action. Llevar can express duration without a gerundio in some contexts.
